records.name: Overlays & Content Scripts
records.purpose: These are the surfaces Tabatha injects into every page you visit: the InBar strip, the Gatekeeper, the BlockGate, and the pause sticky note. Each one is reproduced here from the shipped content-script source, down to the hardcoded colors, radii, and button labels.

records.name: InBar states · Overlays & Content Scripts
records.purpose: The InBar is the one piece of Tabatha that is always on screen, so it is the one that had to be smallest. It sits on the page you are actually reading and states what you said this tab was for. That is the whole job: a tab with a declared purpose is hard to drift away from without noticing , and noticing is the point. The states below are the interesting part - what it looks like when a focus is paused, when a tab has no intent at all, when an agent is driving, and when you have collapsed it down to a nub because you need the pixels back.

records.name: Overlays & prompts · Overlays & Content Scripts
records.purpose: Everything Tabatha injects that interrupts rather than informs. The Gatekeeper is the load-bearing one: open a new tab with no link behind it and it asks "Why are you here?" before the page resolves. Strict mode wants an answer, Relaxed mode takes a shrug. This is deliberate friction, applied at the one moment it is cheap - before you have started - rather than in a report you read afterwards. The rest hold lines you drew earlier: BlockGate on sites you blocked, Welcome Back after time away, the Backburner prompt when you park something you are blocked on.

records.name: InBar — active
records.purpose: The default state. Left: intent timer (cyan) and total task timer (green) flanking the active focus. Center: the tab's intent, with the link glyph showing it is bound to that focus. Right: the full action row.

records.name: InBar — paused
records.purpose: Pausing collapses the bar to an amber strip carrying the first 40 characters of your pause note, so you can see where you left off without expanding anything. The action row drops to resume, note, and collapse.

records.name: InBar — no intent set
records.purpose: With no context and no focus, the timers give way to a single dash and the center becomes a red call to action. Clicking the badge opens the popup so the tab can be given a purpose.

records.name: InBar — agent mode
records.purpose: When an AI agent is driving the tab, the bar takes a violet cast and the time is attributed as agent-driven rather than yours. The pan glyph appears alongside it when Let Me Cook mode is on.

records.name: InBar — nub (collapsed)
records.purpose: Collapsing the bar leaves a 22px nub pinned to the corner. It keeps its state color, so a paused session still reads as amber from across the screen and one click brings the bar back.

records.name: Notes panel
records.purpose: A scratchpad that slides out of the bar and autosaves against the current focus, task, or intent. The note glyph turns amber whenever a note exists, so the bar itself tells you there is something written down.

records.name: Sticky note overlay
records.purpose: Come back to a paused tab and this is what greets you: the note you wrote to yourself, on paper, over the page. The tilt is randomized per note and the whole thing is deliberately analog, because a system dialog is too easy to dismiss without reading.

records.name: InBar edit dropdown
records.purpose: Rewrite the tab's intent or reassign it to another focus without leaving the page. Completed and resolved items are filtered out, and the list sorts active first, then paused, so the likely target is always at the top.

records.name: Backburner prompt
records.purpose: For work that is blocked rather than finished. Name what you are waiting on, set a timer, and pick what you will do instead. Tabatha reopens the loop when the timer expires so the blocked item is never silently dropped.

records.name: Checkpoint card
records.purpose: The periodic progress check. Answering costs one sentence and a single button, and the progress level is what feeds the follow-through data: five honest options including Stuck, because pretending is worse than admitting.

records.name: Gatekeeper — Strict vs Relaxed
records.purpose: The same question in two temperaments. Strict requires an intent before the page loads; Relaxed adds a dismiss path that lets you browse untracked. The badge is the only thing that tells you which contract you are under.

records.name: Gatekeeper — inherited context
records.purpose: Open a link from a tab that already has a context and the child inherits it, pre-filled. The hint above the control makes the inheritance visible so it can be corrected rather than accepted by inertia.

records.name: Gatekeeper — agent selected
records.purpose: The "Who's working?" control defaults to human. Choosing Agent opens a tab-scoped controller span, so the whole intent's time is attributed as agent-driven instead of quietly inflating your own hours.

records.name: BlockGate
records.purpose: A friction wall on blocked sites. Fifty characters of justification and an explicit time box, or take the exit and bank a focus win. The leave button is styled to be the attractive one on purpose.

records.name: Welcome Back overlay
records.purpose: A three-second flash when you return from an idle gap over a minute. It reports the gap and gets out of the way, because the point is to account for the missing time, not to interrogate you about it.

records.name: What's New modal
records.purpose: Fires once per version bump on the new tab page, reading from the parsed changelog. It shows up to three releases so a user who skipped a few still sees everything that changed, and Esc or a click outside dismisses it.

records.name: Focus & Time
records.purpose: The surfaces that hold a single focus and the clock behind it: the FocusBar and its edit, checkpoint, and timeline panels, the queue and backburner that hold what you are not doing yet, and the shift controls that bound the working day. Every string, color, and radius here is lifted from the shipping extension source rather than redrawn.

records.name: Component library · Focus & Time
records.purpose: A focus is one thing you are doing, with a name, a budget, and a clock. This group is the machinery around that idea. FocusBar holds the focus you are on now; FocusQueue and BackburnerDock hold what you are not on yet, which matters because the honest reason a queue exists is that you will be interrupted. The editing surfaces exist because tracked time is only useful if it is true: machines sleep, meetings overrun, and a tracker that cannot be corrected after the fact just teaches you to ignore it.

records.name: FocusBar - active
records.purpose: The one focus you are on right now: funnel stage, tab and window count, elapsed time, switch count, and the tag path it bills to. The timer counts down the budget; every action on the focus lives in one row under it.

records.name: FocusBar - paused / drifted / over-time
records.purpose: Three states the same bar takes. Paused adds an amber left rule and swaps the whole action row for a single Resume. Drifted keeps the timer running but flags that attention left. Over-time turns the timer red and counts up instead of down.

records.name: FocusBar - edit panel
records.purpose: Corrects a focus after the fact. Moving "Started at" earlier backdates it and credits the minutes; the handler bounds the request to your clock-in and reports overlap with other focuses instead of blocking it, since parallel focuses both keep their time.

records.name: FocusBar - checkpoint form
records.purpose: A progress note filed mid-focus. The note is optional except on Stuck, which requires text; the progress level you submit with becomes the entry's glyph on the timeline and feeds the staleness nudge after 30 minutes without a checkpoint.

records.name: FocusInput - empty state
records.purpose: The only way into a focus: a label, a minute budget, and a realm. Submitting with an empty label shakes the card rather than starting an unnamed focus. The mic dictates into the same field; More reveals the full tag setup.

records.name: FocusInput - pending
records.purpose: The in-flight state while the background service worker acknowledges the focus. Inputs dim and the button reads "⏳ Setting…" so a double submit cannot open two focuses. If it never clears, the service worker is stale and needs a reload.

records.name: FocusQueue
records.purpose: Everything set aside but not dropped. The left rule is the funnel color, or amber if paused; a paused row resumes where a queued row switches. Stage and priority are editable in place, and 📊 opens a queued focus's timeline without resuming it.

records.name: BackburnerDock
records.purpose: Focuses parked on purpose, aging in view so they cannot be quietly forgotten. The sub-line names why it went back and how long ago; the three actions are resume, snooze another 10 minutes, or dismiss it for good.

records.name: FocusHistory
records.purpose: Closed focuses, newest first, collapsed to three until expanded. A drifted focus is flagged red rather than hidden, so a session that fell apart still reads as time spent. Elapsed is the credited total, backdating included.

records.name: Now Bar
records.purpose: A one-line answer to "what am I meant to be on". It sits above the focus engine on the dashboard and carries only the label, its priority chip, and its funnel stage. The chip color steps from red through amber to green as priority drops.

records.name: CheckpointTimeline
records.purpose: The audit trail for one focus, in edit mode. Time edits clamp to the wall clock since start and cannot undo the currently running portion, so the preview line shows the real result before commit. Trim removes time tracked since your last browser activity; Remove last pause gives back what a pause took. System entries dim but stay.

records.name: FlipClock
records.purpose: A four-layer split-flap digit: two static halves plus two animated flaps that fold and unfold on change. Bebas Neue with an Outfit fallback. The small variant carries the countdown seconds; pulse-red marks leading zeros as a countdown runs out.

records.name: Shift Controls
records.purpose: The outer boundary on the day: focus time can only exist inside a shift, which is why backdating clamps to clock-in. The ON BREAK chip turns the live timer amber. "1 other open" means another install still has a shift running, and links to Live Stints to resolve it.

records.name: StagePicker
records.purpose: The six funnel stages every focus moves through, as one inline row. The selected chip fills to its own color at 33% alpha; the rest stay outlined and muted. Reused by the FocusBar edit, the queue, the sidebar, and the InBar dropdown.

records.name: Priority picker
records.purpose: The P1-P5 select on every queued focus, expanded here to show all options at once. Color is derived, not stored: P1 and P2 read red, P3 and P4 amber, P5 green. Unset focuses default to P5 rather than demanding a decision up front.

records.name: Data & Analytics
records.purpose: Every chart on this page is hand-rolled: the extension ships no chart library, so each bar, cell and track is a plain div with a CSS width or height. That constraint is the whole aesthetic here, from the 365-day activity heatmap down to the 24 hour-of-day slivers in Peak Productivity Hours.

records.name: Analytics surfaces · Data & Analytics
records.purpose: What the tracking is for. If Tabatha only ever showed you a running clock it would be a stopwatch; these components turn stints into something you can act on - which hours are actually productive, where the day fragments, what a context really costs. The reporting bias is toward patterns over totals , because a weekly total tells you nothing you can change. Worth noting: every chart here is hand-rolled CSS. The extension ships no charting library at all, which keeps the bundle small and the rendering identical across all twelve themes.

records.name: Analytics stat cards
records.purpose: The dashboard's top row: today's focus count and time, a 7-day completion rate, the consecutive-day streak, and open tasks against the total. Each is a glass card with one accent number.

records.name: Top Focuses bar chart
records.purpose: The five most frequent focus labels, ranked. The label sits inside the track and the count rides outside it, so a long label truncates instead of pushing the bar around.

records.name: Time by Category
records.purpose: The same bar primitive as Top Focuses, switched to the secondary accent and sorted by tracked milliseconds. The five categories here sum to the 2h 51m on the stat card above.

records.name: Context Distribution
records.purpose: The Business / Personal / Unset split across every focus item. Unset is deliberately given a dull grey: it is the number the product wants you to drive down.

records.name: ActivityHeatmap
records.purpose: 365 days as 11px cells, seven rows deep, switchable between browser time, browser plus desktop, and follow-through actions. One real quirk is reproduced faithfully: levels 1 to 3 read rgba(var(--accent-rgb, 59,130,246), …) but --accent-rgb is never defined anywhere in the codebase, so the fallback always wins and the mid-levels render blue in every theme. Only the top level picks up the real cyan accent.

records.name: Daily Hours chart
records.purpose: Fourteen days as vertical stacked bars, break time riding on top of work time. Past ten days the chart tightens its gaps and thins the axis to every fifth label. The six active days here total the 31h 12m in the summary below.

records.name: Peak Productivity Hours + Break Patterns
records.purpose: One 24-bar primitive, two readings. Peak hours plots minutes worked per hour of day; break patterns reuses it with a break count and the amber palette. Axis labels print only every sixth hour.

records.name: Weekly comparison
records.purpose: Six weeks of clocked hours, each bar scaled against the busiest week rather than a fixed ceiling. The week label is just the month-day slice of the week key.

records.name: Work analytics summary
records.purpose: The four numbers that head the Work Shifts analytics view. Context switches carry two derived rates, because 63 switches means nothing until you know it happened across 31 hours and 7 shifts.

records.name: Time per Focus + scaffolded stub
records.purpose: Focus time intersected with clocked shifts, ranked. Below it, the product's stub convention: anything scaffolded but not yet wired gets a dashed border, italic amber text and a COMING SOON badge, so the roadmap is visible in the UI instead of hidden in a backlog.

records.name: Reporting row
records.purpose: CSV export is real and runs entirely client-side off the computed analytics. The two dashed buttons are honest about not existing yet.

records.name: LogsPanel filter chips
records.purpose: Nine log types, each with its own glyph and colour, toggling its slice of the table. A type with nothing in it drops to 40% opacity and hides its count, as Audit Trail does here.

records.name: LogsPanel table
records.purpose: Every tracked event in one table, merged from tabs, intents, focuses, clock stints, breaks, contexts, blocks, tasks and the audit trail. The header sticks, focus rows show used against allotted time, and the list pages in rather than rendering thousands of rows at once.

records.name: No shift data yet. Clock in from the Dashboard and analytics will build themselves.
records.purpose: What the Work Shifts analytics view shows before you have ever clocked in. It points at the one action that fills it, and promises nothing else.

records.name: Range selector
records.purpose: Three ranges drive every number on the analytics view, including what the CSV export contains. The active range inverts to a solid accent fill with black text.

records.name: Org & Team
records.purpose: Tabatha starts as a solo attention tool, but the same clock and context data rolls up into orgs, teams, and a five-level work hierarchy. These are the surfaces that make one person's focus legible to their manager, and one manager's roster legible to everyone on it.

records.name: Org & team surfaces · Org & Team
records.purpose: Tabatha runs across more than one browser, machine, and person, and these are the surfaces where that shows. They answer a narrow set of questions: who is working right now, on what, and which shifts were left open by a machine that never clocked out. Live Stints and AbandonedStints exist because of a real failure mode - an install that dies mid-stint leaves a clock running forever, and a ghost shift is worse than a missing one. The schedule surfaces cover the agreement side: required hours, shortfalls, and the requests that change them.

records.name: TeamActivityPanel roster
records.purpose: One row per member, one chip per install of theirs. A manager sees who is clocked in, on break, or drifting, without asking. Chips that miss a heartbeat for five minutes dim to transparent rather than lying about live state.

records.name: StatusChip, all 9 states
records.purpose: A strict precedence ladder: focus state beats clock state, clock state beats presence, and presence beats nothing at all. Only the last three render muted, so a live member never reads as grey.

records.name: Non-manager gate
records.purpose: Read access is enforced server-side, so a regular member would otherwise see an empty panel that looks broken. This note makes the absence deliberate and tells them the way in.

records.name: Generate Invite Token
records.purpose: Minting runs through a security-definer RPC, so only owners and managers can produce a token. One shot, scoped to an org plus optional team, with an explicit role and expiry rather than an open door.

records.name: Pending Invites
records.purpose: Unredeemed, unexpired tokens only. The expiry turns amber inside the last 24 hours so a stale invite gets noticed before it dies, and Revoke kills it for anyone still holding it.

records.name: Organizations + Teams
records.purpose: Membership rows with the caller's role, plus both empty states shown together. Creating an org makes you its owner, which is what unlocks the invite minting panel below it in Settings.

records.name: InitiativesPanel org tree
records.purpose: The full five-level hierarchy in one collapsible tree, with cascade counts at every node and dedicated buckets for clients and projects that never got a parent. Indentation is level times 16 plus 8, so depth is readable at a glance.

records.name: ProjectsClientsPanel
records.purpose: The flat CRUD view over the same hierarchy the tree renders. Three tabs with live counts, inline add, expandable clients that reveal their projects and tasks, and a dimmed Completed shelf so finished work stays visible without competing.

records.name: Live Stints panel
records.purpose: Every install this user owns, sorted live shift first, then abandoned, then idle. This is the fix for ghost stints: an install that clocked in and vanished leaves a row nothing else could reach, so Reconcile rebuilds the shift from its last heartbeat instead of double-counting hours.

records.name: AbandonedStintsModal
records.purpose: The same reconciliation, surfaced at the moment it matters: clocking in again. Freshness is re-checked on open, so an install that came back online is never accused of being abandoned, and the user can always skip through.

records.name: OtherProfilesStrip
records.purpose: Your other installs as chips in the header, each expanding into a read-only view of that device's intent queue. Stale ghosts collapse into one dashed "offline" chip that routes to Live Stints, instead of cluttering the strip with day-old frozen state.

records.name: CompanionStatus
records.purpose: The extension's read on the desktop companion: a glowing dot when the local socket is up, plus the foreground app and its window title. This is what keeps Tabatha from calling you idle while you are heads-down in another program.

records.name: Schedule, 3 modes
records.purpose: SELF, MANAGE, and REQUESTS behind one mode switch, gated by role. A dedicated-hours member cannot edit their own grid, only propose a change; a self-managed one sets their own hours but still owes the minimums.

records.name: ShortfallList + Requests inbox
records.purpose: Missed hours are recorded, not silently forgiven. Each shortfall carries an amber border until it is made up, shifted, or excused with a reason, and a manager clears the resulting proposals from one inbox.

records.name: Settings
records.purpose: Tabatha's settings page is a single React surface with 22 sections behind a sticky left nav, a live sync-health chip, and a keyboard-driven search that jumps straight to a field. Every control below is reproduced from the shipping source, down to the exact labels, placeholder text, and state colors.

records.name: Settings components · Settings
records.purpose: Twenty-two sections, which is a lot, and the reason is that Tabatha is opinionated by default and those opinions have to be negotiable. Nearly every piece of friction in the product - gate strictness, timers, blocked sites, retention - is a setting here, because friction you cannot tune is friction you eventually disable entirely . That volume is also why the search exists: press / and fuzzy-find any option rather than hunting the nav. The theme system renders live in the preview column, so you can see a change before you keep it.

records.name: ⚙️ Settings
records.purpose: The complete shipped section list: all 22 sections, in source order. The 220px rail is sticky and carries the version stamp, the sync-health pill with manual sync and reload buttons, and the search box. The active section gets a 3px accent rule.

records.name: Sync health chip
records.purpose: One pill, five states, derived from the last successful sync timestamp and the connection state. It lives in the nav header so cloud health is visible from every section without opening Sync & Account.

records.name: SettingsSearch
records.purpose: Press / anywhere on the page to focus it. It indexes every field label across all 22 sections, groups hits by their section, and jumps to and highlights the field on select. Arrow keys move the selection.

records.name: Appearance theme select
records.purpose: All 12 shipped themes, shown expanded. Picking one rewrites the CSS custom properties at the document root, so every surface (popup, sidebar, new tab, InBar) reskins at once.

records.name: Theme swatches
records.purpose: The real token triple behind each theme: page background, accent-primary, accent-secondary. Four of the twelve are light themes, which is why every component ships contrast-checked against both ends.

records.name: Field row primitives
records.purpose: The two atoms every section is built from: a flex label-and-control row with a hairline rule, and a 36x20 toggle whose knob slides from 2px to 18px. Section labels break long panels into groups.

records.name: Focus Engine funnel stages
records.purpose: The six stages a focus moves through, listed read-only so the stage keys and their colors are discoverable. The same colors drive the funnel chips on the dashboard and in the sidebar.

records.name: URL Rules
records.purpose: Three pill tabs over one section. A rule maps a wildcard URL pattern to a default intent and context, so matching tabs are classified on creation instead of prompting the Gatekeeper. The empty state is shown for reference.

records.name: Domain Groups
records.purpose: Every domain Tabatha has seen, with visit and path counts, how many tabs are open on it now, and whether a rule already covers it. Star a domain to target it, dismiss one to hide it from the list. Two actions are still stubs.

records.name: No intent changes recorded yet.
records.purpose: An audit trail of every re-declared intent, oldest strikethrough on the left and the new one in accent. Useful for spotting a domain you keep reclassifying, which is usually a missing URL rule.

records.name: Developer panel
records.purpose: The in-extension console. Every service tags its output with a source, so a hung message or a failed sync is traceable without opening the service worker inspector. Level filter, auto-refresh, and a clear button.

records.name: Data retention
records.purpose: Time Tracking holds the two thresholds that drive idle detection and the context nudge, plus the retention window. A daily alarm prunes companion activity past the cutoff; 0 disables pruning.

records.name: CortexPanel status & config
records.purpose: Cortex watches how work actually happens and proposes changes. This panel proves it is running (frame counts, folder, last write) and exposes the routing ladder: local harness first, backend proxy next, hosted gateway and bring-your-own-key still gated.

records.name: ⌨️ Bind a hotkey for the tab you reopen most
records.purpose: 🧩 Generate a snippet for the report header you retype executed

records.name: Webhooks
records.purpose: Push any of 13 lifecycle events to an endpoint you control, with an optional shared secret for signature verification. This is how Tabatha feeds time entries and focus state into other tools without an integration.

records.name: About & changelog
records.purpose: Version, codename, and ecosystem, over a changelog rendered from the shipped release feed. It opens on the current release and expands to the full history, so users can see what changed without leaving the extension.

records.name: Primitives & Panels
records.purpose: The building blocks under every Tabatha surface: buttons and glass cards, the autocomplete and tagging inputs that bind a focus to its hierarchy, and the full-panel overlays for commands, shortcuts, tasks, and timelines. Every string, colour, and radius here is lifted from the shipping source, including the places where the product hardcodes a value off the scale.

records.name: Component library · Primitives & Panels
records.purpose: The shared vocabulary. Every surface in Tabatha is assembled from these, which is why the product feels consistent without a component framework doing the enforcing - there is no design-system dependency here, just a small set of pieces used everywhere. GlassCard , PopButton and the pickers carry the Pop-Art palette and the halved 2/4/8 radius scale. The global overlays are also here: the command palette, the fourteen keyboard shortcuts, and the link/merge modal that resolves the duplicate contexts you inevitably create.

records.name: GlassCard + PopButton
records.purpose: The two base primitives. GlassCard is a translucent blurred panel; PopButton is the Pop-Art call to action with a hard black border and an offset magenta drop shadow, in three sizes.

records.name: ComboInput
records.purpose: A free-text field with an inline suggestion list: type to filter existing entities, arrow to highlight, or fall through to the italic create row when nothing matches. It is the input behind every Client, Project, and Task field.

records.name: TagPicker
records.purpose: Binds a focus to Realm › Client › Project › Task. Collapsed it is a dashed affordance that becomes a breadcrumb once filled; expanded it exposes the realm toggle and three ComboInputs, with autofill up the hierarchy.

records.name: StagePicker
records.purpose: The FUNNEL_STAGES row on a focus. The active chip tints to its own stage colour at 20% alpha; the rest stay transparent and muted. A compact variant drops the labels for tight rows.

records.name: VoiceInput
records.purpose: A round mic toggle that dictates into any field. Listening turns the button red and pulses a ring, streaming an interim transcript beside it; permission failures surface inline rather than as a modal.
records.keywords: idle sm / md listening transcript error disabled

records.name: Tooltip
records.purpose: A flat dark label placed above or below its trigger, capped at 280px so long hints wrap instead of stretching the row. No caret, no arrow, just offset and shadow.

records.name: CommandPalette
records.purpose: Ctrl+K opens one search field over nine fixed actions plus live focus, task, and tab results. The selected row is marked by an accent left rule and a faint accent wash, so the keyboard cursor stays readable at a glance.

records.name: KeyboardShortcuts (ShortcutsHelp)
records.purpose: The full binding map, reachable from anywhere with Ctrl+/. Fourteen rows cover the palette, focus and break control, theme, all eight dashboard tabs, and Escape as the universal dismiss.

records.name: LinkMergeModal
records.purpose: One modal, three modes: attach a tab to an existing intent, spin up a new focus for it, or merge two intents together. It hardcodes its own 4px and 6px radii instead of reading the 2/4/8 scale, reproduced here exactly as shipped.
records.keywords: link mode create mode + empty state

records.name: TasksPanel
records.purpose: Task CRUD with its own TASK_STAGES funnel, distinct from the focus funnel. Each row can start an intent, be edited inline, be linked to focuses (the count shows as a badge), or be deleted; completed rows dim and strike through.

records.name: Tab-list rows
records.purpose: Each tracked tab: category glyph from CATEGORY_ICONS, title, its Context, and time-in-context. The left rule colors by priority; a 🔒 marks a tab locked to its context, and paused tabs mute their timer.

records.name: PriorityPill
records.purpose: A 1-to-10 priority read as three bands: red for the top three, amber through six, green below. An unsynced item shows a muted P— rather than guessing a rank.

records.name: ChangelogView
records.purpose: Renders the shipped changelog in-product. Section icons are matched by a substring of the section label, so an entry only needs plain headings; inline code spans pick up JetBrains Mono on a surface chip.

records.name: UnifiedTimeline
records.purpose: The desktop companion's OS-level activity for the day, laid out as one proportional bar. Categories carry fixed colors, breaks get a diagonal hatch, and green and red rules mark clock-in and clock-out.

records.name: The Gatekeeper Store shot
records.purpose: "Why are you here?" - good friction before every undirected new tab. Continue, Side Quest, Sugar Box, or Park.
records.keywords: gatekeeper intent popup inpop overlay strict relaxed why are you here friction side quest sugar box park

records.name: Sidebar Store shot
records.purpose: Active focus, elapsed time, priority-coded tab list.
records.keywords: sidebar command center panel focus tabs queue groups stash tasks

records.name: New Tab Dashboard Store shot
records.purpose: Quick Access, working state, Return to Flow.
records.keywords: home new tab dashboard quick access return to flow flipclock heatmap greeting

records.name: Sync & Account Store shot
records.purpose: Cloud Sync health chip and org, metadata only.
records.keywords: settings sync account cloud org health chip privacy metadata

records.name: Backdating Store shot
records.purpose: Credited minutes, overlaps kept, checkpoint timeline.
records.keywords: backdating time editing checkpoint timeline overlap credited minutes clock-in

records.name: Quick Switch
records.purpose: The 400×500 toolbar popup: search every tab by its Context, not just its title.
records.keywords: popup quick switch search tabs ctrl space toolbar feedback 400x500

records.name: Work Shifts · Analytics
records.purpose: Daily hours, peak productivity, break patterns, time per focus. Every chart is hand-rolled CSS.
records.keywords: work shifts analytics charts daily hours peak breaks per focus csv weekly live stints schedule

records.name: Settings · All 22 Sections
records.purpose: The full nav, the Appearance section, and the live-preview column with all 12 themes.
records.keywords: settings all sections nav appearance theme preview swatches 22 developer cortex

records.name: Surfaces
records.purpose: A surface is a whole place in Tabatha you can be: the gate that interrupts a new tab, the sidebar that replaces the browser's own tab strip, the dashboard that replaces the new-tab page. They exist because an attention tool cannot live in one panel - the moment you need to declare intent is not the moment you need to review your week, and neither belongs in a popup. Each frame below is the real UI at the size it actually ships, not a mockup of it.

records.name: Component library
records.purpose: The surfaces above are assembled from these ninety components, grouped by the job they do rather than by where they happen to render. Most are shown in several states , because the states are where the product's opinions live: a focus that has drifted, a stint left open by a machine that went to sleep, a queue with nothing in it. Each card names the file it comes from, so what you read here can be checked against the source.

records.name: Unified Calendar
records.purpose: A real month, week, and day calendar inside Tabatha, with your focuses and stints on it, plus a sidebar agenda for the day ahead. The data layer is built; the calendar UI and the account connection are what remain.

records.name: URL Rules Intelligence
records.purpose: Tabatha learns which sites belong to which context instead of making you write every rule by hand. The first pass of the rules engine shipped; the training mode that suggests rules from your own history is in progress.

records.name: Activity timeline editing
records.purpose: Correct a tracked day directly on the timeline: trim a range, split a block, mark a break that the tracker recorded as work.

records.name: Intent to Focus pipeline
records.purpose: Promote an intent you declared at the Gatekeeper into a tracked focus without retyping it, so the thing you said you were doing becomes the thing being measured.

records.name: Asana integration
records.purpose: Link an Asana task to a focus so time tracked in the browser lands against the task it belongs to. Detection and linking work; the setup guide and a status indicator in Settings are outstanding.

records.name: Chrome Web Store listing
records.purpose: Tabatha currently installs and self-updates through its own signed channel. Publishing to the Web Store is the remaining distribution step.

records.name: Smart deferral and task splitting
records.purpose: Defer a task to a future stint and let Tabatha find and size the slot from your real schedule, instead of pushing a date and hoping.

records.name: Backburner
records.purpose: Park a focus you are blocked on, switch to something else honestly, and get reminded to come back to it rather than quietly dropping it.

records.name: Calendar auto-backfill
records.purpose: Turn calendar events you actually attended into time entries after the fact, so meeting-heavy days do not have to be reconstructed from memory.

records.name: Meeting block detection
records.purpose: Recognise when you are in a meeting and switch tracking into meeting time automatically, instead of logging it as browsing.

records.name: Priority matrix and lazy priority
records.purpose: Two tiers of priority: a fast P1 to P5 pass for everyday sorting, and an urgency-versus-relevance matrix for when the fast pass is not enough.

records.name: Voice input
records.purpose: Speak an intent or a note instead of typing it, on the fields where typing is the thing that stops you from logging at all.

records.name: InPop intent switcher
records.purpose: Make the in-page popup header a full intent switcher, so changing what a tab is for does not require opening another surface.

records.name: Priority challenge interrupts
records.purpose: Varied, fatigue-resistant prompts that ask whether this is genuinely the most important thing right now. Under consideration: interrupts that get ignored are worse than no interrupts.

records.name: Focus and task model cleanup
records.purpose: Normalise the distinction between a focus and a task, and make promoting one into the other a first-class path rather than a convention.

records.name: Org-wide analytics
records.purpose: Cross-member analytics for org admins, building on the per-person analytics that already ship.

records.name: Parallel focuses
records.purpose: Hold more than one focus at once for genuinely concurrent work. Deliberately blocked on the focus and task model cleanup landing first, because building it on the current model would be rework.

records.name: Multi-track timeline editing
records.purpose: A deeper editor over the activity timeline with multiple tracks and contribution notes. Waiting on the single-track timeline editing work to finish first.

records.name: Survives a restart
records.purpose: The extension no longer disappears after a computer restart. It installs to a stable, self-healing location instead of a build folder.

records.name: Backdating moves the start
records.purpose: Setting an earlier start time always takes effect. Overlapping work is reported back to you instead of being silently refused.

records.name: Honest time-edit limits
records.purpose: When an edit is capped by your clock-in or by the current time, the editor says so and offers the earliest time it can accept.

records.name: Self-hosted update channel
records.purpose: Installs update themselves in the background, on login and every six hours, with a verified download and no store review in the loop.

records.name: Component showcase
records.purpose: Every major surface and component rendered from the real design system. The page you are reading is the site it grew into.

records.name: Work schedule profiles
records.purpose: Per-person profiles for dedicated hours or self-managed time, with required-hours floors, a weekly schedule editor, and a change-request flow.

records.name: Shortfall tracking
records.purpose: Under-hours periods are recorded at clock-out and can be made up, shifted, or excused, rather than vanishing.

records.name: Schedule view
records.purpose: Three modes in Work Shifts: your own schedule, team roster management, and an approval inbox for incoming requests.

records.name: Work Shifts analytics
records.purpose: Daily hours, weekly comparison, peak productivity hours, break patterns, time per focus, context switching, and CSV export. Every chart is hand-rolled CSS; the extension ships no charting library.

records.name: Time-editing overhaul
records.purpose: Type a duration like 500m or 8h20m to set, add, or subtract time, with a live preview and Enter to apply.

records.name: Offline-gap detector
records.purpose: If your machine was asleep or off while tracking, Tabatha spots the gap on wake and asks you to confirm, credit, or adjust it. No phantom hours.

records.name: Abandoned stint recovery
records.purpose: Stints orphaned by a previous session surface when you next clock in, with an end-time picker to correct them.

records.name: Settings search
records.purpose: Press / in Settings to fuzzy-find any option across all 22 sections. Status chips click through to the panel that owns them.

records.name: Storage quota fix
records.purpose: Fixed a quota problem where pause, resume, and other changes appeared to work but were never written to disk.

records.name: Cloud Sync reliability
records.purpose: Account and display-name changes are queued durably and retried instead of hanging, and signing in now syncs automatically.

records.name: Live Stints accuracy
records.purpose: Live Stints no longer shows ghost cards for installs that are gone, and no longer silently does nothing right after startup.
